content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Tom ___ (have) a car, he would drive to work every day instead of taking the bus.
distractor_rationale: would is never used in the if-clause of a second conditional; this is the most common learner error
present simple has signals a real, likely condition — a first conditional — not a hypothetical one
will have signals a first conditional about a real future possibility, not an imaginary present situation

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If I were taller, I ___ (join) the basketball team at school.
distractor_rationale: will join is used in a first conditional for a realistic future outcome, not a hypothetical one signalled by were
joined is past simple and cannot serve as the result clause verb in a conditional structure
would have joined is the result clause of a third conditional about an unreal past situation, not an unreal present one

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Maria ___ (travel) around the world if she had enough money saved in her account.
distractor_rationale: will travel belongs to a first conditional expressing a realistic future result, but had signals a hypothetical condition
travels is a present simple form used for habits or facts, not for a hypothetical result clause
would have travelled is the result clause of a third conditional about an unreal past event, not an unreal present situation

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If she ___ (be) in charge of the office, she would change the meeting schedule immediately.
distractor_rationale: was is the informal spoken form; the formal subjunctive were is required here — this tests the were/was contrast directly
would be incorrectly places would in the if-clause, which is a classic learner error in second conditionals
is signals a first conditional with a real present condition, contradicting the hypothetical meaning of would change

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We would spend more time outdoors if the weather ___ (not be) so cold and rainy every afternoon.
distractor_rationale: would not be incorrectly puts would in the if-clause; would belongs only in the result clause
is not signals a first conditional about a realistic present condition, but would spend signals a hypothetical situation
was not is the informal spoken alternative; the formal subjunctive were not is the target form being tested here

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If David knew the answer, he ___ (tell) us right now instead of looking it up online.
distractor_rationale: will tell is the result clause of a first conditional about a real possibility, but knew signals an imaginary present situation
would have told is the result clause of a third conditional about an unreal past event, not an unreal present one
tells is present simple, used for habits or facts, and cannot function as the result clause of a conditional

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If the supermarket ___ (stock) that brand of coffee, I would buy it every week without fail.
distractor_rationale: would stock incorrectly places would in the if-clause; this is the most frequent second conditional error
stocks is present simple and creates a first conditional about a real situation, not a hypothetical one
had stocked is past perfect and belongs in the if-clause of a third conditional about an unreal past event

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If I ___ (be) you, I would talk to the manager about the problem before it gets worse.
distractor_rationale: was is the informal spoken alternative; the fixed expression if I were you requires the formal subjunctive were
would be incorrectly introduces would into the if-clause, which violates second conditional grammar
am is present simple and turns the sentence into an impossible first conditional, since the speaker cannot literally be the listener

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna would feel much less stressed about the exam if she ___ (not have) so many other assignments due at the same time.
distractor_rationale: would not have incorrectly places would in the if-clause; this is the classic second conditional learner error
has not had is present perfect and cannot form the if-clause of a second conditional describing a current hypothetical situation
had not had is past perfect and belongs in the if-clause of a third conditional about an unreal past event, not an unreal present one

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If the new sports centre ___ (be) closer to our neighbourhood, we would probably go there three times a week.
distractor_rationale: was is the informal spoken variant; the formal subjunctive were is the target form being tested in this written context
would be incorrectly places would in the if-clause, which is a fundamental second conditional error
is creates a first conditional about a real present condition, but would go signals that the situation is imaginary and unlikely

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If I were taller, I ___ (join) the basketball team at my local sports centre.
distractor_rationale: will join belongs to the first conditional and signals a real future outcome, not a hypothetical one
joined is the past simple and cannot function as the result clause of a second conditional without would
would have joined is the result clause of a third conditional about an unreal past situation, not an unreal present one

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If she ___ (be) in charge of the project, she would organise the schedule very differently from her manager.
distractor_rationale: was is the informal spoken form but the subjunctive were is required in formal written second conditional, which this item tests
would be in the if-clause is the classic learner error of placing would in the condition rather than the result
is signals a real present condition belonging to the first conditional, not a hypothetical situation

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Mark would probably ___ (travel) abroad more often if flights were not so expensive for his family.
distractor_rationale: travelled is the past simple and cannot follow would in the result clause; would requires the base infinitive
be travelling creates would be travelling, a continuous form that does not fit the habitual frequency implied by more often
have travelled creates would have travelled, the result clause of a third conditional about an unreal past event, not an unreal present one

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Anna ___ (speak) a second language fluently, she would apply for the international sales position that opened last month.
distractor_rationale: would speak in the if-clause is the most frequent learner error; would belongs only in the result clause of a second conditional
speaks is present simple and turns the sentence into a first conditional about a real, achievable condition rather than an imaginary present ability
had spoken is the past perfect used in third conditional if-clauses referring to unreal past events, not unreal present abilities

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If she ___ more money, she would buy a bigger flat.
distractor_rationale: 'would have' in the if-clause is a classic learner error — 'would' never appears in the if-clause of a second conditional
'has' uses present simple, which belongs to the first conditional for realistic situations, not hypothetical ones
'would had' is grammatically impossible in any conditional structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If I knew the answer, I ___ tell you straight away.
distractor_rationale: 'will' belongs to the first conditional for likely future situations, not hypothetical ones signalled by past simple 'knew'
'am going to' expresses a real intention about the future and cannot pair with the hypothetical past simple 'knew'
'do' is a present simple auxiliary and cannot form a result clause in any conditional structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom would cycle to work every day if the roads ___ safer.
distractor_rationale: 'would be' in the if-clause is the typical error of putting 'would' in the condition rather than the result clause
'are' uses present simple, making this a first conditional about a realistic possibility rather than a hypothetical one
'will be' also belongs to first conditional structures for likely future events, not hypothetical present situations

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If I ___ you, I would apologise to her before the meeting tomorrow.
distractor_rationale: 'was' is the informal/non-standard form; the formal subjunctive 'were' is required in the fixed expression 'if I were you'
'am' uses present simple, which signals a real condition and belongs to the first conditional, not a hypothetical one
'would be' places 'would' in the if-clause, which is a core structural error in second conditional formation

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If Anna spoke better English, she ___ apply for the international job without any hesitation.
distractor_rationale: 'will' signals a realistic first conditional expectation, but the past simple 'spoke' marks this as a hypothetical situation
'would have' forms the result clause of a third conditional (past hypothetical), not a second conditional about the present
'is going to' expresses a real future plan and cannot follow the hypothetical past simple 'spoke'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: We ___ travel abroad more often if flights were not so expensive.
distractor_rationale: 'will' belongs to first conditional structures describing realistic outcomes, but 'were' signals a hypothetical second conditional
'would have' is the result form for the third conditional about past events, not a present hypothetical
'do' is a present simple auxiliary and cannot function as a modal result in any conditional structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If the company ___ its employees better, fewer people would leave every year.
distractor_rationale: 'would pay' incorrectly places 'would' in the if-clause instead of the result clause, a core second conditional error
'pays' uses present simple, turning this into a first conditional about a real possibility rather than a hypothetical complaint
'has paid' uses present perfect, which describes a completed past action and cannot set up a hypothetical present condition

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If he ___ closer to the city centre, he would not need to drive to work every morning.
distractor_rationale: 'would live' places 'would' in the if-clause, which is the most common structural error in second conditional formation
'lives' uses present simple, making this a first conditional about a real situation rather than an imaginary present one
'had lived' forms the if-clause of a third conditional about a past event, not a hypothetical present situation

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If it were not raining so heavily right now, we ___ sit outside and have lunch in the garden.
distractor_rationale: 'will' signals a realistic first conditional expectation, but 'were not raining' marks this clearly as a hypothetical second conditional
'could' expresses ability or possibility rather than the direct hypothetical result that 'would' provides in a standard second conditional
'would have' forms the result clause of a third conditional about a past event, not a present hypothetical situation

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria ___ feel much less stressed if she worked four days a week instead of five.
distractor_rationale: 'will' belongs to the first conditional for realistic or likely outcomes, but 'worked' signals a hypothetical second conditional situation
'would have' is the result form used in third conditional sentences about unreal past events, not present hypotheticals
'is going to' expresses a real, planned future event and is incompatible with the hypothetical past simple 'worked'

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If she would have more free time, she would learn to play the guitar.
content.error_sentence: If she would have more free time, she would learn to play the guitar.
content.corrected_sentence: If she had more free time, she would learn to play the guitar.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If I was a doctor, I would work in a hospital in a small town.
content.error_sentence: If I was a doctor, I would work in a hospital in a small town.
content.corrected_sentence: If I were a doctor, I would work in a hospital in a small town.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If Mark will win the competition, he would buy a new bicycle for his son.
content.error_sentence: If Mark will win the competition, he would buy a new bicycle for his son.
content.corrected_sentence: If Mark won the competition, he would buy a new bicycle for his son.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: They would move to a bigger apartment if they will earn a higher salary.
content.error_sentence: They would move to a bigger apartment if they will earn a higher salary.
content.corrected_sentence: They would move to a bigger apartment if they earned a higher salary.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If he was in charge of the project, he would organise the meetings more carefully.
content.error_sentence: If he was in charge of the project, he would organise the meetings more carefully.
content.corrected_sentence: If he were in charge of the project, he would organise the meetings more carefully.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If Anna would speak better English, she would apply for the international position at her company.
content.error_sentence: If Anna would speak better English, she would apply for the international position at her company.
content.corrected_sentence: If Anna spoke better English, she would apply for the international position at her company.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If the weather is warmer this weekend, we would have a picnic in the park with the children.
content.error_sentence: If the weather is warmer this weekend, we would have a picnic in the park with the children.
content.corrected_sentence: If the weather were warmer this weekend, we would have a picnic in the park with the children.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: David would saved a lot of money every month if he cooked at home instead of eating out.
content.error_sentence: David would saved a lot of money every month if he cooked at home instead of eating out.
content.corrected_sentence: David would save a lot of money every month if he cooked at home instead of eating out.

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Tom ___ (have) a car, he would drive to work every day instead of taking the bus.
distractor_rationale: would is never used in the if-clause of a second conditional; this is the most common learner error
present simple has signals a real, likely condition — the first conditional — not a hypothetical situation
will have signals a first conditional about a real future possibility, not an imaginary present scenario

duplicate: exact duplicate of a prior item
